is head of the [diocese of Durham](/wiki/Diocese_of_Durham "Diocese of Durham") in the [province of York](/wiki/Province_of_York "Province of York"). The diocese is one of the oldest in England and its bishop is a member of the [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords"). [Paul Butler](/wiki/Paul_Butler_(bishop) "Paul Butler (bishop)") was the most recent bishop of Durham until his retirement in February 2024.

is an [episcopal](/wiki/Episcopal_polity "Episcopal polity") title which takes its name after the [tidal island](/wiki/Tidal_island "Tidal island") of [Lindisfarne](/wiki/Lindisfarne "Lindisfarne"), which lies just off the northeast coast of [Northumberland](/wiki/Northumberland "Northumberland"), England. The title was first used by the [Anglo-Saxons](/wiki/Anglo-Saxons "Anglo-Saxons") between the 7th and 10th centuries. In the reign of Æthelstan (924–939) Wigred, thought by Simon Keynes to have been Bishop of [Chester-le-Street](/wiki/Chester-le-Street "Chester-le-Street"), attested royal charters.[[3]](#cite_note-3) According to George Molyneaux, the church of [St Cuthbert](/wiki/St_Cuthbert "St Cuthbert") "was in all probability the greatest landholder between the Tees and the Tyne".[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMolyneaux201530-4) Traditionally, following the chronology of the twelfth-century writer [Symeon of Durham](/wiki/Symeon_of_Durham "Symeon of Durham"), historians have believed that the body of St Cuthbert and centre of the diocese lay at Chester-le-Street from the ninth century until 995, but recent research has suggested that the bishops may have been based at [Norham](/wiki/Norham "Norham") on the [River Tweed](/wiki/River_Tweed "River Tweed") until after 1013.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWoolf2018232–33-5)[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGuigan2022121–62-6) The title of "[bishop of Lindisfarne](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Bishop_of_Lindisfarne "Roman Catholic Bishop of Lindisfarne")" is now used by the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") for a [titular see](/wiki/Titular_see "Titular see") (there is also a Catholic titular see of [Cuncacestre](/wiki/Cuncacestre "Cuncacestre"), the Latin form of Chester-le-Street).

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Diocesan bishop in the Church of England

"Bishop of Lindisfarne" redirects here. For the modern titular see, see [Roman Catholic Bishop of Lindisfarne](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Bishop_of_Lindisfarne "Roman Catholic Bishop of Lindisfarne").

The **bishop of Durham** is head of the [diocese of Durham](/wiki/Diocese_of_Durham "Diocese of Durham") in the [province of York](/wiki/Province_of_York "Province of York"). The diocese is one of the oldest in England and its bishop is a member of the [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords"). [Paul Butler](/wiki/Paul_Butler_(bishop) "Paul Butler (bishop)") was the most recent bishop of Durham until his retirement in February 2024.

The bishop is officially styled *The Right Reverend (First Name), by Divine Providence Lord Bishop of Durham*, but this full title is rarely used. In signatures, the bishop's [family name](/wiki/Family_name "Family name") is replaced by *Dunelm*, from the Latin name for Durham (the Latinised form of [Old English](/wiki/Old_English "Old English") *Dunholm*). In the past, bishops of Durham varied their signatures between *Dunelm* and the [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") *Duresm*. Prior to 1836 the bishop had significant [temporal powers](/wiki/State_(polity) "State (polity)") over the [liberty of Durham](/wiki/Liberty_of_Durham "Liberty of Durham") and later the [county palatine of Durham](/wiki/County_Palatine_of_Durham "County Palatine of Durham"). The bishop, with the [bishop of Bath and Wells](/wiki/Bishop_of_Bath_and_Wells "Bishop of Bath and Wells"), escorts the sovereign at the [coronation](/wiki/Coronation_of_the_British_monarch "Coronation of the British monarch").

[Durham Castle](/wiki/Durham_Castle "Durham Castle") was a residence of the bishops from its construction in the 11th century until 1832, when it was given to the [University of Durham](/wiki/Durham_University "Durham University") to use as a college. [Auckland Castle](/wiki/Auckland_Castle "Auckland Castle") then became the bishops' main residence until July 2012, when it was sold to the Auckland Castle Trust. The bishop continues to have offices there.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bishop_of_Durham&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The **bishop of Lindisfarne** is an [episcopal](/wiki/Episcopal_polity "Episcopal polity") title which takes its name after the [tidal island](/wiki/Tidal_island "Tidal island") of [Lindisfarne](/wiki/Lindisfarne "Lindisfarne"), which lies just off the northeast coast of [Northumberland](/wiki/Northumberland "Northumberland"), England. The title was first used by the [Anglo-Saxons](/wiki/Anglo-Saxons "Anglo-Saxons") between the 7th and 10th centuries. In the reign of Æthelstan (924–939) Wigred, thought by Simon Keynes to have been Bishop of [Chester-le-Street](/wiki/Chester-le-Street "Chester-le-Street"), attested royal charters.[[3]](#cite_note-3) According to George Molyneaux, the church of [St Cuthbert](/wiki/St_Cuthbert "St Cuthbert") "was in all probability the greatest landholder between the Tees and the Tyne".[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMolyneaux201530-4) Traditionally, following the chronology of the twelfth-century writer [Symeon of Durham](/wiki/Symeon_of_Durham "Symeon of Durham"), historians have believed that the body of St Cuthbert and centre of the diocese lay at Chester-le-Street from the ninth century until 995, but recent research has suggested that the bishops may have been based at [Norham](/wiki/Norham "Norham") on the [River Tweed](/wiki/River_Tweed "River Tweed") until after 1013.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWoolf2018232–33-5)[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGuigan2022121–62-6) The title of "[bishop of Lindisfarne](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Bishop_of_Lindisfarne "Roman Catholic Bishop of Lindisfarne")" is now used by the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") for a [titular see](/wiki/Titular_see "Titular see") (there is also a Catholic titular see of [Cuncacestre](/wiki/Cuncacestre "Cuncacestre"), the Latin form of Chester-le-Street).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/England_diocese_map_pre-925.svg/250px-England_diocese_map_pre-925.svg.png)](/wiki/File:England_diocese_map_pre-925.svg)

The Anglo-Saxon dioceses before 925

The Anglo-Saxon bishops of Lindisfarne were [ordinaries](/wiki/Ordinary_(officer) "Ordinary (officer)") of several [early medieval](/wiki/Early_medieval "Early medieval") [episcopal sees](/wiki/Episcopal_see "Episcopal see") (and [dioceses](/wiki/Diocese "Diocese")) in [Northumbria](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Northumbria "Kingdom of Northumbria") and pre-[Conquest](/wiki/Norman_Conquest "Norman Conquest") [England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England"). The first such see was founded at [Lindisfarne](/wiki/Lindisfarne "Lindisfarne") in 635 by [Saint Aidan](/wiki/Aidan_of_Lindisfarne "Aidan of Lindisfarne").[[7]](#cite_note-7)

From the 7th century onwards, in addition to their spiritual authority, the bishops of Lindisfarne, and then Durham, also acted as the civil ruler of the region as the lord of the [liberty of Durham](/wiki/Liberty_of_Durham "Liberty of Durham"), with local authority equal to that of the king. The bishop appointed all local officials and maintained his own court. After the [Norman Conquest](/wiki/Norman_Conquest "Norman Conquest"), this power was retained by the bishop and was eventually recognised with the designation of the region as the [County Palatine of Durham](/wiki/County_Palatine_of_Durham "County Palatine of Durham"). As holder of this office, the bishop was both the [earl](/wiki/Earl "Earl") of the county and bishop of the diocese. Though the term 'prince-bishop' has become a common way of describing the role of the bishop prior to 1836, the term was unknown in medieval England.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

A UNESCO site describes the role of the bishops as a "buffer state between England and Scotland":[[9]](#cite_note-9)

> From 1075, the bishop of Durham became a prince-bishop, with the right to raise an army, mint his own coins, and levy taxes. As long as he remained loyal to the king of England, he could govern as a virtually autonomous ruler, reaping the revenue from his territory, but also remaining mindful of his role of protecting England's northern frontier.

A 1788 report adds that the bishops had the authority to appoint judges and barons and to offer pardons.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Except for a brief period of suppression during the [English Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War"), the bishopric retained this temporal power until it was abolished by the [Durham (County Palatine) Act 1836](/wiki/Durham_(County_Palatine)_Act_1836 "Durham (County Palatine) Act 1836") ([6 & 7 Will. 4](/wiki/6_%26_7_Will._4 "6 & 7 Will. 4"). c. 19) with the powers returned to the Crown.[[11]](#cite_note-11) A shadow of the former temporal power can be seen in the bishop's coat of arms, which contains a coronet as well as a mitre and crossed crozier and sword. The bishop of Durham also continued to hold a seat in the House of Lords; that has continued to this day by virtue of the ecclesiastical office.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## Assistant bishops

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bishop_of_Durham&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Assistant bishops")]

Among those who have served as assistant bishops of the diocese have been:

* 1889–1902 (ret.): [Daniel Sandford](/wiki/Daniel_Sandford_(Bishop_of_Tasmania) "Daniel Sandford (Bishop of Tasmania)"), Rector of [Boldon](/wiki/Boldon "Boldon") and [coadjutor bishop](/wiki/Coadjutor_bishop "Coadjutor bishop"); former [Bishop of Tasmania](/wiki/Anglican_Bishop_of_Tasmania "Anglican Bishop of Tasmania")[[24]](#cite_note-24)
* 1904–1906: [Noel Hodges](/wiki/Noel_Hodges "Noel Hodges"), former [Bishop of Travancore and Cochin](/wiki/Bishop_of_Travancore_and_Cochin "Bishop of Travancore and Cochin") (later Assistant Bishop [of Ely](/wiki/Assistant_Bishop_of_Ely "Assistant Bishop of Ely") and [of St Albans](/wiki/Assistant_Bishop_of_St_Albans "Assistant Bishop of St Albans"))[[25]](#cite_note-25)
* 1970–1975: [Kenneth Skelton](/wiki/Kenneth_Skelton "Kenneth Skelton"), Rector of [Bishopwearmouth](/wiki/Sunderland_Minster "Sunderland Minster") and former [Bishop of Matabeleland](/wiki/Bishop_of_Matabeleland "Bishop of Matabeleland") (became [Bishop of Lichfield](/wiki/Bishop_of_Lichfield "Bishop of Lichfield"))[[26]](#cite_note-26)
